content.text: users are being harmed right now

— not when an internal system component crosses a threshold that may or may not affect users. The distinction matters more for GenAI services than for traditional APIs because the stack is deep: your service calls a provider, the provider calls a model, the model calls a safety filter, and a cache may intercept any of those calls. An alert on provider API latency may fire while users see perfectly fast responses from a warm cache.
This topic covers the principles behind symptom-based alerting, how they translate to the four GenAI signal categories, and the concrete Prometheus alert rules that implement them.

attrs.alt: Symptom-based vs cause-based alerting for GenAI — taxonomy of four symptom categories

content.text: defines the distinction clearly: alert on
(what is broken internally). For a GenAI service:
CPU can spike during inference with no latency impact
A fallback may be serving; the symptom is what counts
P99 latency > SLO budget burn rate
Low cache hits don't matter if latency is fine
Provider latency is internal; model streaming hides it
Occasional rate limits may be absorbed by retry logic
would a user notice if this alert fires and nobody responds for one hour?
If yes, it is a symptom alert. If no, it is a cause alert and belongs in a dashboard, not a pager.
a meaningful fraction of requests are failing
. The threshold is derived from the SLO error budget burn rate, not an arbitrary percentage.
For a 99.5 % availability SLO, a 5× burn rate means the error budget will be exhausted in 1/5 of the SLO window. If the window is 30 days, the budget drains in 6 days — page-worthy.

# alerts/genai-availability.yaml
groups:
  - name: genai_availability
    rules:
      # Page: fast burn — 5× error budget burn rate over 5m
      - alert: GenAIHighErrorRateFastBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
          ) > 0.025   # 5× of 0.5% SLO
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'GenAI error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} (5× SLO burn)'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-high-error-rate'

      # Ticket: slow burn — 2× error budget burn rate over 1h
      - alert: GenAIHighErrorRateSlowBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[1h]))
            /
            sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[1h]))
          ) > 0.010   # 2× of 0.5% SLO
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'GenAI error rate slow burn — {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}'

pattern avoids both alert fatigue (from threshold-too-low) and missed incidents (from threshold-too-high). A 5-minute spike at 2× burn rate may resolve itself; a 1-hour sustained 2× burn rate is draining the budget and warrants investigation.
For GenAI-specific error subtypes, alert separately on
(upstream failures the team can't fix) vs

- alert: GenAIProviderErrorSurge
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod", error_type="provider_error"}[10m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[10m]))
          > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: genai-oncall
          escalate_to: platform-oncall   # platform owns provider relationships
        annotations:
          summary: 'Provider errors at {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} of requests'

      - alert: GenAIContentFilterSurge
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod", error_type="content_filter"}[15m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[15m]))
          > 0.10   # content filter blocks >10% is likely a prompt regression
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Content filter blocking {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} — possible prompt regression'
, not internal component times. For a GenAI service the user-visible latency depends on whether the feature is
— a user who sees the first token stream within 2 seconds tolerates a 20-second full response much better than 8 seconds of silence

groups:
  - name: genai_latency
    rules:
      - alert: GenAIHighLatencyFastBurn
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum by (le) (
              rate(genai_request_duration_seconds_bucket{env="prod"}[5m])
            )
          ) > 8.0   # SLO: P99 < 4s; 8s = 2× SLO budget
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'P99 latency {{ $value }}s (2× SLO budget of 4s)'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-high-latency'

      - alert: GenAIStreamingTTFTHigh
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum by (le) (
              rate(genai_ttft_seconds_bucket{env="prod"}[5m])
            )
          ) > 3.0   # SLO: TTFT P95 < 1.5s
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Streaming TTFT P95 is {{ $value }}s (SLO: 1.5s)'
for streaming requests. This metric records total generation time, which can be 20+ seconds for long responses. It is
what the user perceives as latency. Use
GenAI-specific saturation alerts cover two distinct dimensions:
(a business constraint). Both manifest as user-visible harm when they trigger.

groups:
  - name: genai_saturation
    rules:
      # Rate limit saturation — users receive 429 Too Many Requests
      - alert: GenAIRateLimitSaturation
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_rate_limit_events_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
          > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: '{{ $value | humanizePercentage }} of requests hitting rate limits'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-rate-limit'

      # Quota pre-warning — 80% of TPM quota consumed
      - alert: GenAIQuotaPreWarning
        expr: |
          100 * sum(rate(genai_tokens_total{env="prod", provider=~".+"}[1m]))
            /
          scalar(genai_rate_limit_tokens_per_minute{env="prod"})
          > 80
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Token quota {{ $value | humanize }}% consumed — rate limiting imminent'

      # Spend budget burn rate
      - alert: GenAISpendFastBurn
        expr: |
          sum(increase(genai_cost_usd_total{env="prod"}[1h])) > 500
        for: 0m   # no 'for' — fires immediately; spend is already gone
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Hourly GenAI spend ${{ $value }} exceeds $500 threshold'
on the spend alert is intentional: spend that has already occurred cannot be reversed. The alert fires immediately to prevent compounding over the next hour.
Quality alerts are unique to AI systems. They fire when the
correctness or usefulness of responses degrades
— a failure mode that has no equivalent in traditional APIs.
Because quality is measured asynchronously via eval pipelines, quality alerts have a
(rarely page; usually ticket). The exception is a sudden large drop that correlates with a model deployment.

groups:
  - name: genai_quality
    rules:
      # Sustained quality score degradation vs 7-day baseline
      - alert: GenAIEvalScoreDrift
        expr: |
          (
            avg_over_time(genai_eval_score{env="prod"}[2h])
            -
            avg_over_time(genai_eval_score{env="prod"}[7d] offset 1h)
          ) < -0.15
        for: 2h
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Eval score dropped {{ $value | humanize }} from 7-day baseline'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-eval-drift'

      # Hallucination rate spike (requires LLM-as-judge hallucination scorer)
      - alert: GenAIHallucinationRateHigh
        expr: |
          avg_over_time(
            genai_eval_score{env="prod", eval_dimension="hallucination_free"}[1h]
          ) < 0.80
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Hallucination-free rate dropped to {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}'

      # Negative user feedback surge
      - alert: GenAINegativeFeedbackSurge
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_feedback_total{env="prod", sentiment="negative"}[30m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_feedback_total{env="prod"}[30m]))
          > 0.25
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'Negative feedback at {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} of rated requests'
pattern is the recommended structure for SLO-linked alerts. It creates two alert pairs — one short-window (fast detection) and one long-window (sustained burn detection) — for each golden signal.
Slow burn — drain in ~15 days without fix
The two-window structure reduces false positives: a 5-minute spike that resolves before the 1-hour confirmation window does not page. The short and long windows must
exceed the burn rate threshold for a page to fire.

# MWMBR template for availability
- alert: GenAISLOBurnRateCritical
  expr: |
    (
      sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
    ) > 0.025   # 5× burn rate
    and
    (
      sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[1h]))
      / sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[1h]))
    ) > 0.025
  for: 2m
  labels:
    severity: critical
  annotations:
    summary: 'Error budget fast burn: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} error rate (both 5m and 1h windows)'
Alert routing for GenAI services requires two dimensions:
(application team vs platform team vs ML team).

# alertmanager/routing.yaml (excerpt)
route:
  group_by: ['alertname', 'env', 'team']
  group_wait: 30s
  group_interval: 5m
  repeat_interval: 4h
  receiver: default-receiver
  routes:
    - match:
        severity: critical
        team: genai-oncall
      receiver: genai-pagerduty
      continue: true   # also send to Slack
    - match:
        severity: critical
        escalate_to: platform-oncall
      receiver: platform-pagerduty
    - match:
        severity: warning
        team: ml-oncall
      receiver: ml-slack-channel
    - match:
        severity: warning
      receiver: genai-slack-channel
Every alert that pages must have a runbook. The runbook should answer the questions the on-call engineer asks in the first 5 minutes:
What is the user impact? (How many users, which features, what are they seeing?)
What is the most common cause? (With links to dashboards pre-filtered to the incident time range)
What is the immediate mitigation? (Not fix — mitigation. E.g. fall back to a simpler model, disable a feature flag, scale up replicas)
Who is the escalation contact for each cause branch?
What data must be collected for the post-incident review?

Runbook quality rule: if an on-call engineer who has never seen this system before cannot follow the runbook to stop user harm within 10 minutes, the runbook needs to be rewritten. Test runbooks during game days before production incidents reveal the gaps.
Pages fire for single transient errors
Use rate() with a minimum for: duration; never alert on raw counts
Use symptom (user error rate) not cause (health endpoint)
Every alert needs for: ≥ 2m; use 5m for non-critical
Alert stops being accurate after traffic grows
Express thresholds as multiples of the SLO budget burn rate
On-call has to search Confluence during an incident
CI should reject alerts without a runbook_url annotation
Low-traffic services never alert; high-traffic services always do

Symptom-based alerting for GenAI services organises around four user-visible failure modes: availability, latency, cost/saturation, and quality. Each translates to Prometheus alert rules that reference SLO error budget burn rates rather than internal thresholds.
Alert only when users are being harmed — filter cause-based alerts to dashboards
Use multi-window multi-burn-rate pairs: fast burn (5m/1h) for pages, slow burn (30m/6h) for tickets
For streaming features, alert on TTFT not total generation time
Quality alerts fire on eval score drift and negative feedback, not on model internals
Every page-worthy alert requires a runbook URL as an annotation; CI enforces this
Route by both severity and team ownership — provider errors escalate differently than application bugs
